text: Athletes accustomed to high chronic training loads have fewer injuries than athletes training at lower workloads, a phenomenon termed the Training-Injury Prevention Paradox.
Team sport athletes who performed more than 18 weeks of training before sustaining their initial injury were at reduced risk of sustaining a subsequent injury.
Non-contact soft-tissue injuries are primarily caused by excessive and rapid increases in training loads rather than by high training load per se.
The acute:chronic workload ratio is the best practice predictor of training-related injuries and should be used to guide load prescription.
Training load should be measured up to twice daily and tracked over periods of weeks and months to accurately capture injury risk.
Under-training may increase injury risk, meaning workload reductions are not always protective against injury.
Both internal and external training loads should be captured alongside acute and chronic timeframes to best represent an athlete's training burden and injury risk.
